Environmental noise screening tool v2.0 — March 2026
This tool is currently under construction. Features and data may be incomplete or subject to change.
A screening tool to predict noise levels at nearby receivers. Select source and receiver locations on the map, enter noise source data, and the tool will indicate potential compliance. Depending on location, the criteria will be automatically calculated or can be entered manually.
Disclaimer: While every reasonable effort has been made to ensure that all the information displayed and provided in this application is correct, Resonate Consultants do not make any guarantees as to the completeness, reliability or accuracy of the information and disclaim any and all liability to any person/s. Any actions you take based on the information you find within this application are strictly at your own discretion and risk. This tool is not designed to provide definitive predictions or confirm compliance. It serves only as a preliminary screening tool to indicate the potential for compliance. For a reliable assessment and formal advice, please contact Resonate Consultants. By using this tool, you consent to this disclaimer and agree to its terms.

Map

Click the map to place the noise source and up to 4 receiver locations. Straight-line distances are calculated automatically and entered in the table below. Markers can be dragged to fine-tune positions.

VIC assessment parameters

Applies −1 dB per 100 m (rounded down, max 9 dB) when source zone ≠ receiver zone.
Reset to auto
0 = fully Type 1 (residential), 1 = fully Type 3 (industrial). Per Figure 1 of Pub 1826.5, IF is derived from zones within 140 m / 400 m circles around each noise sensitive area (NSA). Leave blank to auto-derive per NSA, or enter a value to override all.

Intrusiveness: 35
Minimums: Day 35, Evening 30, Night 30. Intrusiveness = RBL + 5 dB (Table 2.1) — residential receivers only.

Day = 7 am–10 pm Night = 10 pm–7 am INL INL = Indicative Noise Level as defined in the Environment Protection (Commercial & Industrial) Noise Policy 2023 Clause 5. It is the averaged value of the Indicative Noise Factors for the applicable land use categories used to determine environmental noise criteria. Day = 7 am–6 pm (Mon–Sat) Evening = 6 pm–10 pm Night = 10 pm–7 am (& all day Sun) Criteria = EPA Pub 1826.5 Zone levels from Table 1 (urban) or Annex B (rural) of EPA Publication 1826.5, adjusted for distance to zone boundary and background noise classification. Minimum limits: 45/40/35 dB(A) D/E/N (urban), 45/37/32 dB(A) (rural). Night cap: 55 dB(A). Night Lmax criterion based on NSW EPA Day = 7 am–6 pm (Mon–Sat) / 8 am–6 pm (Sun) Evening = 6 pm–10 pm Night = 10 pm–7 am Residential: min(Intrusiveness, Amenity) | Non-residential: Amenity only Residential: trigger = lower of intrusiveness (RBL + 5 dB) and amenity criterion (Table 2.2 ANL − 5 dB + 3 dB facade correction). Non-residential (incl. hotel/motel): intrusiveness does not apply (NPI §2.1); trigger = amenity criterion only. NSW Noise Policy for Industry (2017). Emergency equipment:

Emergency equipment criteria

Criteria based on the intent of the Environment Protection (Commercial & Industrial) Noise Policy 2023, the testing time and operation of the equipment.

MBS 010

The application of MBS 010 will be triggered if the subject site is located in an ANEF contour or is within a Noise Attenuation Area (Noise and Air Emissions Overlay). SAPPA ↗
Relevant Layers Relevant information Distance to site Indicative SEC
ANEF———
ANR———
Noise Attenuation Area———
Designated Roads———
Rail———
Mixed land use areas———
* Road name could not be automatically identified from the speed zone data. SEC is based on an assumed speed of 50–60 km/h and should be confirmed by the user against actual posted speed limits and designated road classification.
Notes on MBS 010 screening methodology

Triggering: MBS 010 applies if the site is within an ANEF contour (Aircraft Noise Exposure Forecast) or a Noise Attenuation Area (Noise and Air Emissions Overlay) as identified in the Planning and Design Code. Overlay data is queried from SAPPA (location.sa.gov.au). If neither overlay applies, designated road/rail/mixed use rows show N/A.

Distances to roads: Measured as the straight-line distance from the nearest point on the site boundary (cadastral parcel) to the nearest edge of the designated road corridor polygon, plus 3 m. Per MBS 010, separation distance for roads is measured from a point 3 m inside the road corridor boundary (i.e. 3 m from the corridor edge to the road surface).

Distances to rail: Measured as the straight-line distance from the nearest point on the site boundary to the closest rail line (centreline). Per MBS 010, separation distance for rail is measured from the closest rail line, not the corridor boundary.

Road polygons: Designated Road Polygon data (Type A, B, R) is sourced from SAPPA (Planning & Building > Planning Reference > Designated Road Polygon). Using local GeoJSON files (road_type_a/b/r.geojson). Check SAPPA for confirmation of coverage.

Road names: Road names are sourced from the SA Department for Infrastructure and Transport Speed Zones MapServer (layer 12). The nearest road segment within 200 m of the closest road corridor point is identified and its RDNAME attribute is used.

Road speeds: Posted speed limits are queried from the SA DIT Speed Zones MapServer at the point on the road corridor nearest to the site. If the road name and speed cannot be automatically identified (marked with *), an assumed speed of 50–60 km/h is used for SEC calculation and should be confirmed by the user against actual posted speed limits.

SEC calculation: Sound Exposure Categories are determined per MBS 010 Tables 4.1 (Type A & B roads), 4.2 (Type R roads), and 4.3 (rail). Distance thresholds vary by road type and speed band (50–60, 70–90, 100–110 km/h). For ANR contours, SEC is per Table 4.4 based on the ANR dB(A) value. Mixed land use areas are assigned SEC 1 per Clause 4.3.3 where the zone includes non-residential land uses.

Rail data: Train and tram line data is sourced from SA government shapefiles (centreline geometry).

ANR contours: Aircraft Noise Reduction contours are sourced from local GeoJSON data (Adelaide, Parafield, RAAF Edinburgh airports).

Limitations: Distances are straight-line only and do not account for terrain, barriers, or building shielding. All values are indicative — verify with SAPPA and consult an acoustic engineer for detailed assessment. The governing SEC for a building element is the highest SEC from all applicable sound sources (Clause 4.2.2).

Derivation of noise emission criteria

Description Day Evening Night

Propagation method

Select the sound propagation model. The simple method uses geometric spreading only (−20 log10 d − 8 dB). ISO 9613-2 adds frequency-dependent atmospheric absorption (ISO 9613-1) and ground attenuation per octave band (§7.3.1 detailed method with source, receiver, and middle region contributions).
Source heights use library defaults and can be adjusted per source row when visible. Lmax predictions always use the simple method.
Notes on ISO 9613-2 implementation

Standard: Acoustics — Attenuation of sound during propagation outdoors — Part 2: General method of calculation (ISO 9613-2:1996).

Overall formula: For each octave band f (63 Hz to 8 kHz):
Lp(f) = Lw(f) − A(f),   where   A(f) = Adiv + Aatm(f) + Agr(f)

Geometric divergence (Adiv): Point source, free-field spherical spreading per Equation 8:
Adiv = 20 log10(d) + 11  dB

Atmospheric absorption (Aatm): Per octave band, calculated from ISO 9613-1 using temperature and humidity:
Aatm(f) = α(f) × d / 1000  dB
where α(f) is the absorption coefficient in dB/km. Default values at 10 °C, 70% RH: [0.1, 0.4, 1.0, 1.9, 3.7, 9.7, 32.8, 117.0] dB/km for [63, 125, 250, 500, 1k, 2k, 4k, 8k] Hz. For other conditions, the full ISO 9613-1 formula is used (O2 and N2 relaxation frequencies, saturation pressure, molar concentration).

Ground attenuation (Agr): Frequency-dependent, detailed method per §7.3.1, Table 3:
Agr(f) = As(f) + Ar(f) + Am(f)
Source region As and receiver region Ar use height-dependent functions a′(h), b′(h), c′(h), d′(h) for 125–1000 Hz bands, scaled by ground factor G. At 63 Hz, As = Ar = −1.5 dB (ground reflection). At 2k–8k Hz, As = −1.5(1−G).
Middle region Am applies when d > 30(hs+hr), with factor q = 1 − 30(hs+hr)/d.
Negative Agr values represent ground reflection amplification (e.g. hard paved surfaces). On soft porous ground (G=1), Agr can exceed 10 dB at 250–500 Hz (ground dip effect).

Ground factor G: G=0 for hard reflective surfaces (concrete, asphalt, water); G=0.5 for mixed; G=1 for soft porous ground (grass, soil, rural land). A single G value is used for all three regions in this tool.

Band summation: The overall A-weighted predicted level is the energetic sum across all octave bands:
LAeq = 10 log10 Σ 10Lp(f)/10  dB
Source spectra in the library are pre-A-weighted, so no additional A-weighting correction is applied.

Multi-source combination: Individual source predictions are combined energetically (Equation 3). Each source’s level is adjusted for quantity (10 log10 n) and operational time fraction (10 log10(t/T)).

Barrier attenuation: Barrier screening is calculated from drawn barriers and OSM building footprints using the Maekawa diffraction formula with path-length difference δ. Screening is applied per octave band for ISO 9613-2, or as a broadband approximation for the simple method. Three diffraction paths are computed (over top + two lateral ends) per ISO/TR 17534-3 §5.2. The 20 dB cap applies to over-top Dz only (§5.3); lateral values are uncapped.

Barrier Dz geometry: Uses the Fresnel zone path-length difference (z = 2a²/(dss+dsr)) where a is the perpendicular distance from the diffraction edge to the source–receiver line. ISO/TR 17534-3 barrier test cases T08 and T09 validated within ±1.0 dB.

Directivity (Dc): All sources are treated as omnidirectional (Dc = 0). Source directivity is not modelled.

Barrier screening (Abar): Building and barrier screening uses the Maekawa diffraction formula with Fresnel zone path-length difference, computed per octave band. Three diffraction paths (over top + two lateral ends) are energy-summed per ISO/TR 17534-3 §5.2. The 20 dB (single) / 25 dB (double diffraction) cap applies to the over-top path only — lateral paths are uncapped.

Terrain screening: Per-band Maekawa diffraction applied to the single dominant ridgeline detected from DEM (Open-Elevation API). Uses the same Fresnel zone δ formula as building barriers. Where both building and terrain screening exist, the greater per-band insertion loss is applied (no double-counting).

Miscellaneous (Amisc): Not implemented. No industrial-site screening or housing/vegetation attenuation is included.

Convergence with simple method: On hard ground (G=0) with no atmospheric absorption, Agr = −3 dB at all bands, so the ISO formula reduces to Lw − 20 log10(d) − 8, matching the simple hemispherical spreading method.

Contour map: The noise contour map uses an adaptive grid (5–50 m resolution based on zoom level) with Chaikin curve smoothing applied to contour boundaries. Grid point results may differ slightly from discrete receiver calculations due to interpolation.

Ground factor: A single ground factor G is applied uniformly to source, receiver, and middle regions. The calculation engine supports per-region factors (Gs, Gr, Gm) but the UI currently exposes only a single value.

Limitations: This is a screening-level implementation. For detailed assessment, use dedicated acoustic modelling software (e.g. SoundPLAN, CadnaA, iNoise) with site-specific terrain, barriers, building reflections, and meteorological corrections (Cmet).

Scenarios

Summary of which assessment periods each source is active in, based on entered Lw data. A tick indicates the source has Lw data entered for that period.

Source contribution

Predicted sound pressure level at each receiver from individual sources, using the selected propagation method. Levels include ground effect, atmospheric absorption, and terrain screening where applicable.

Characteristic penalties

Apply a characteristic penaltyThe presence of an annoying characteristic attracts a decibel penalty under clause 13(3) of the Noise Policy. A penalty is applied because noise with such dominant characteristics are more noticeable and annoying when compared to a constant, broadband or steady noise. For a penalty to be applied, the Noise Policy requires the characteristic to be fundamental to the nature and impact of the noise. The characteristic should dominate the overall noise impact, rather than simply be part of it. Noise characteristics include tonal, impulsive, low frequency, intermittent and modulating. Refer to SA EPA Noise Policy Guideline (2023) for details on noise characters. to the predicted Leq levels before comparison with criteria. The penalised level is shown in the results table.
Modifying factor corrections from Fact Sheet C of the Noise Policy for Industry are added to the predicted noise level before comparison with project noise trigger levels. Applicable corrections include tonal (+2 or +5 dB), low-frequency (+2 or +5 dB), and intermittent (+5 dB, night only). Maximum combined correction: 10 dB.
Characteristic adjustments are added to the predicted noise level per EPA Publication 1826.5. Applicable characteristics include tonal (2–5 dB), impulsive (2–5 dB), and intermittent (3–5 dB). Maximum total adjustment: 10 dB.
Enter the applicable characteristic penalty (dB) to add to the predicted level for each period.

GIS Export

Export assessment data for use in GIS applications (QGIS, ArcGIS, Google Earth).
GeoJSON — recommended for QGIS and ArcGIS. Tip: to get a Shapefile, open GeoJSON in QGIS → Export → Save Features As → ESRI Shapefile.
KML — use with Google Earth or Google Maps.
CSV — receivers only, for Excel or data joins.

How to use

1
Set the site location
Search for an address using the search bar, or click the map to centre the view on your site. The address is used in exported filenames.
2
Place noise source(s)
Click “+ Add source” to create a source. Click “Place” within the source card, then click the map to place the source pin. Each source has its own equipment list and height setting. Multiple sources are energy-summed at each receiver.
3
Enter equipment details
For each source, add equipment items with their sound power level (Lw, dB(A)) and quantity. Use manufacturer data sheets or measured values. If operational time is less than 15 minutes per assessment period, enter the expected duration to apply a time weighting.
4
Place receivers
Click the receiver button (R1–R4) on the toolbar, then click the map. Up to four receivers can be assessed simultaneously. Assign a name to each receiver for reporting purposes.
5
Set zones and receiver types
Use the zone dropdown for each receiver to set the applicable noise zone, or rely on auto-detection from SAPPA (SA), NSW Planning Portal, or VIC zone data. Select the receiver type (e.g. residential, commercial) to apply the correct criteria.
6
Review results
The results table shows predicted levels for day, evening, and night periods against the applicable criteria. Cells highlighted in red indicate an exceedance. Click any receiver row to see a per-band ISO 9613-2 calculation breakdown. Where terrain screening is enabled and elevation data is available, terrain diffraction loss is automatically applied — a mountain icon (⛰) will appear next to predicted levels. Use the terrain profile view in each receiver card to inspect the propagation path.
7
Add buildings, barriers, and building sources (optional)
Activate Buildings to load editable OSM building footprints, or use New building and Barrier to draw custom elements. Use Building source to draw a noise-radiating building polygon where internal noise levels are known — enter the interior Lp, wall/roof construction, and operating periods to calculate radiated facade and roof sound power. All buildings, barriers, and building sources are included in the acoustic calculation.
8
Enable terrain screening (optional)
Click the Terrain button to fetch ground elevation data for all placed pins. The Geoscience Australia 5 m LiDAR DEM is used where available; Open-Elevation (SRTM ~30 m) is the fallback. Where terrain protrudes above the direct line of sight between a source and receiver, diffraction loss is calculated using the Maekawa formula. A terrain indicator appears in the results table where terrain screening is applied. Enable Contours to overlay terrain elevation contour lines on the map.
9
Enable the noise map (optional)
Toggle the Noise Map button to display predicted contours across the site. Select the period (day/evening/night/Lmax), receiver height, and grid size. Finer grids (1–10 m) give more detail but take longer to compute — use Auto for most assessments. Enable the Terrain button before generating the noise map to apply terrain diffraction screening to the contours. Contours are calculated using the same propagation method as the receiver predictions.
10
Load planning overlays (optional)
Use the toolbar to add contextual layers: SA Planning zones (Show Zones), aerial imagery (Aerial), cadastral boundaries (Cadastral), and OpenStreetMap building footprints (Buildings). MBS 010 Screening identifies whether receiver locations fall within a Noise and Emissions Overlay under the SA Planning and Design Code.
11
Export
Save session: downloads a JSON file to reload this assessment later — all sources, receivers, settings, and results are preserved.
GIS Export: exports sources, receivers, and contours as GeoJSON, KML, or CSV for use in QGIS, ArcGIS, or Google Earth.
Screenshot: use your browser or operating system screenshot tool to capture the map at the appropriate zoom level for use as a figure in reports.
Always cross-check predicted results against hand calculations for at least one receiver before using outputs in a formal report.

Methodology

Sound pressure level prediction
This calculator provides an indication of the sound pressure level at a specified distance due to various noise sources. Two propagation methods are available: a simple inverse square law screening method (no ground or atmospheric corrections), and ISO 9613-2 (which includes geometric divergence, ground effect, and atmospheric absorption based on temperature and humidity). It is intended as a screening tool to determine whether more accurate noise modelling and advice should be provided by an acoustic consultant.
Building sources
Building sources model noise radiating from industrial or commercial building facades and roofs where the internal noise level (Lp) is known. The user draws a building polygon on the map, enters the building height, internal Lp per period, and wall and roof construction (Rw). The radiated sound power per facade or roof panel is calculated as Lw = Lp − Rw + 10·log10(S) + 6 dB, where S is the facade or roof area. Facade elements (roller doors, louvres, glazing) with their own Rw can be added per wall. Propagation from the resulting sub-sources uses the same method as point sources.
Barrier attenuation
Barrier screening is calculated from user-drawn barriers and OSM building footprints. The tool uses the Maekawa diffraction formula based on path-length difference to determine insertion loss, computed per octave band. Three diffraction paths are energy-summed: over the barrier top and around each lateral end. The over-top path is capped at 20 dB (single diffraction) or 25 dB (double diffraction); lateral paths are uncapped. A line-of-sight check ensures no attenuation is applied when the direct ray clears the barrier top.
LAmax barrier and terrain screening
LAmax barrier and building attenuation is calculated using the Maekawa diffraction formula applied per octave band to the source Lmax spectrum. Ground effect (Agr) is not applied to LAmax predictions. Terrain screening is applied where enabled. Barrier and terrain screening is applied regardless of whether the simple or ISO 9613-2 propagation method is selected.
Facade reflections
Facade reflections are calculated using the image source method per ISO 9613-2 §7.5. For each source–receiver pair, building and barrier edges within 50 m of the receiver are tested as reflecting surfaces. The reflected contribution from the dominant surface is calculated as a separate propagation path (using the same propagation method as the direct path) and energy-added to the direct path. A single dominant reflection is considered per source–receiver pair. Grazing incidence is excluded (incidence angle >80° relative to surface normal). Hard surface reflection (0 dB loss) is assumed for all buildings and barriers. Reflections are enabled via the Reflections toolbar button and apply to both receiver predictions and the noise map.
Operating % of period
The calculation provides an option to enter the percentage of the assessment period during which each source is operating. A 10·log10(pct/100) weighting is applied to the source level: 100 % → 0 dB, 50 % → −3 dB, 25 % → −6 dB.
Multiple sources — energy summation
Where more than one noise source is present, contributions at each receiver are combined by energy summation: Ltotal = 10·log10(Σ 10Li/10). The combined level will always be higher than the loudest individual source, but by no more than 3 dB when two equal sources are present.
Noise map
The noise map generates a predicted sound level grid across the map extent using the selected propagation method. Grid resolution is adaptive (5–50 m based on zoom level). Contour lines are interpolated from the grid and represent equal noise level lines in dB(A). The noise map uses the same source inputs, propagation method, and receiver height as the receiver predictions. Contour accuracy degrades at the edges of the grid and at large distances from sources.
Zone detection
Receiver zones are detected automatically by querying state planning portal APIs based on the receiver map location: SA uses the SA Planning Portal (SAPPA), VIC uses the Victorian Planning Authority API, and NSW uses the NSW Planning Portal. Zone detection requires an internet connection. Users should verify zone classifications independently, as APIs may not reflect recent planning amendments. Zone information is used to apply the applicable noise criteria for that receiver.
Terrain screening
Where ground elevation data is available, the terrain profile between each source and receiver is sampled from DEM data. The Geoscience Australia 5 m LiDAR WCS service is queried as the primary source; Open-Elevation (SRTM ~30 m resolution) is used as a fallback where WCS data is unavailable. The dominant intervening ridgeline is identified and diffraction loss calculated using the Maekawa formula (identical to the building barrier method). Only the single dominant path is assessed; multiple diffraction is not modelled. Where terrain and building barriers coincide, the greater per-band insertion loss is applied.
Code quality and validation
ISO/TR 17534-3 validation
The core propagation calculations are validated against the reference test cases defined in ISO/TR 17534-3:2015 — Acoustics: Software for the calculation of sound outdoors. This technical report defines input geometries and reference output values for testing implementations of ISO 9613-2. The in-app “Run validation” button (in the Propagation method panel) executes the following ground attenuation test cases:
  • T01: Reflecting ground (G = 0) — reference 44.29 dB — pass (±0.05 dB)
  • T02: Mixed ground (G = 0.5) — reference 41.53 dB — pass (±0.05 dB)
  • T03: Porous ground (G = 1.0) — reference 39.14 dB — pass (±0.05 dB)
The full automated unit test suite (CAT tests, accessible via the developer console) additionally covers T04 (spatially varying ground — pass) and T08/T09/T11 (barrier + ground combined cases). T08, T09, and T11 currently produce results within ±1.0 dB of the ISO/TR 17534-3 reference totals but do not meet the strict ±0.1 dB tolerance due to the combined ground–barrier interaction term. These cases are noted as partial conformance. Users can run the in-app validation at any time via the “Run validation” button.
Terrain screening validation
No terrain-specific test cases exist in ISO/TR 17534-3. The terrain diffraction implementation is validated by regression test: for a flat terrain profile (no elevation variation), the tool is required to return 0 dB terrain insertion loss for all source–receiver pairs. This is confirmed automatically as part of the validation suite.
Security
User-entered data (source names, receiver names) is escaped before insertion into the interface using DOM-native escapeHTML() to prevent cross-site scripting (XSS). No user data is transmitted to any server — all calculations run entirely in the browser.
Conformity statement
This tool implements ISO 9613-2:1996 (Attenuation of sound during propagation outdoors — Part 2: General method of calculation) and is partially conformant with ISO/TR 17534-3:2015 based on the test cases listed above. The GoI-form (Geometry of Implementation) identifies the following implementation status:
  • Ground effect (§7.3): fully implemented, per-band
  • Atmospheric absorption (§7.2): fully implemented
  • Geometric divergence (§7.1): fully implemented
  • Barrier / building screening (§7.4): implemented, single dominant path, top and lateral diffraction
  • Terrain screening: partial — single dominant ridge, per-band Maekawa diffraction, DEM from Geoscience Australia WCS LiDAR (5 m, primary) with Open-Elevation SRTM fallback
  • Meteorological correction (§8): not implemented — this tool is intended for worst-case screening assessments; meteorological corrections are appropriate for detailed assessments only
Limitations
This tool is a screening tool intended to indicate whether more detailed assessment is warranted. It should not be used as a substitute for a full acoustic assessment by a qualified acoustic consultant. Predicted levels should be verified against site measurements before use in formal reports. Results are sensitive to source power level inputs — users are responsible for the accuracy of Lw values entered.
Data sources
Planning zones
Planning zone classifications are detected automatically via state planning portal APIs based on the placed receiver location. The data source varies by jurisdiction:
  • SA: SA Planning Portal (SAPPA) — SA Planning and Design Code
  • VIC: Victorian Planning Authority — Planning Schemes Online
  • NSW: NSW Planning Portal — Environmental Planning Instruments (LEPs and SEPPs)
Zone detection requires an internet connection. Users should verify zone classifications independently as APIs may not reflect recent planning amendments or interim provisions.
Noise criteria
Applicable noise criteria are applied based on the detected zone and the selected state. Criteria sources by jurisdiction:
  • SA: Environment Protection (Commercial and Industrial Noise) Policy 2023 (SA EPA)
  • VIC: Environment Protection Act 2017 and Environment Protection Regulations 2021 (effective 1 July 2021). Noise limits and assessment methodology are set out in EPA Publication 1826.5 — Noise limit and assessment protocol for the control of noise from commercial, industrial and trade premises and entertainment venues. Note: the previous SEPP N-1 framework no longer applies from 1 July 2021.
  • NSW: Noise Policy for Industry (NSW EPA, 2017) — the current recommended guideline for new developments. The NSW Industrial Noise Policy (2000) may still apply to some existing developments and premises under transitional arrangements. Users should confirm which policy applies to their assessment with the NSW EPA.
Users should confirm the applicable policy version and any site-specific conditions with the relevant environment protection authority.
Aerial imagery
Aerial imagery is provided by Esri World Imagery. Imagery currency varies by location — recent development or vegetation change may not be reflected.
Terrain elevation
Ground elevations are fetched from two sources: the Geoscience Australia 5 m LiDAR DEM via WCS (primary, covering most of Australia) and the Open-Elevation API serving SRTM data (~30 m resolution) as a fallback. Elevation data is used to determine effective source and receiver heights above sea level and to calculate terrain diffraction loss along propagation paths. The 5 m LiDAR DEM provides substantially better accuracy in areas of complex terrain. Accuracy of both sources may be reduced in areas of dense vegetation or built-up urban areas (where canopy or building returns affect the DEM surface).
State LiDAR/DEM resources for reference:
  • SA: Surveying and Spatial SA (SAILIS)
  • VIC: DataVic (Vicmap Elevation)
  • NSW: NSW Spatial Services (Elvis elevation portal)
Buildings
Building footprints are sourced from OpenStreetMap (OSM) via the Overpass API. Coverage varies by location — major urban areas generally have good coverage; regional and rural areas may be incomplete. Building heights where shown are sourced from OSM tags and may not be available for all structures. For critical assessments, verify building dimensions against survey data or local government records.
Cadastral boundaries
Property boundaries are sourced from the Esri World Boundaries and Places layer. For definitive cadastral information consult the relevant state land registry:
  • SA: Surveying and Spatial SA (SAILIS / Land Services SA)
  • VIC: Land Use Victoria (Landata)
  • NSW: NSW Land Registry Services (NSW LRS)
MBS 010 screening
MBS 010 (Ministerial Building Standard 010 — Construction requirements for the control of external sound) is a South Australian building standard published under the Planning, Development and Infrastructure Act 2016, available on the PlanSA website. It sets deemed-to-satisfy construction requirements for reducing the intrusion of road, rail, and aircraft noise into habitable rooms of residential buildings (Class 1, 2, 3, Class 4 and Class 9c aged care buildings).
Application of MBS 010 is triggered by the Noise and Emissions Overlay in the SA Planning and Design Code. The current version is Version 3 (1 May 2023). The screening tool queries the SAPPA API to determine whether a receiver location falls within a Noise and Emissions Overlay area. Users should confirm overlay applicability and the current version of MBS 010 with the relevant consent authority.
